message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://artificialanalysis.ai/text-to-speech/leaderboard/selected-voice
**Title:** Text to Speech Leaderboard — Artificial Analysis (Speech Arena)
**Found via:** academic/benchmark — MOS, license, model size search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- cloudflare-one: Guides Cloudflare One Zero Trust and SASE work across Access, Gateway, WARP, Tunnel, Cloudflare WAN, DLP, CASB, device posture, and identity. Use when designing, configuring, troubleshooting, or reviewing Cloudflare One deployments. Retrieval-first: use current Cloudflare docs/API schemas instead of embedded product docs.
- cloudflare-one-migrations: Plans migrations from Zscaler ZIA/ZPA, Palo Alto, legacy VPN, SWG, or SASE stacks to Cloudflare One. Use for migration assessments, policy mapping, rollout plans, and parity/gap analysis.
- durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- turnstile-spin: Set up Cloudflare Turnstile end-to-end in a project — scan the codebase, create the widget via the Cloudflare API, deploy the managed siteverify Worker, write the frontend snippets, validate, and persist the skill. Load this when a user asks to add Turnstile, set up CAPTCHA, protect a form from bots, or fix a Turnstile integration. Mirrors developers.cloudflare.com/turnstile/spin.
- web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the page content and analyze it.

input.prompt: Extract the Text-to-Speech leaderboard data. List the top TTS models ranked, their quality/ELO/MOS scores, and any mention of: Kokoro, XTTS, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Zonos, open-source vs proprietary models, licenses, model sizes, and how the ranking/scoring methodology works (e.g. crowdsourced human preference / arena ELO). Also report any publish date or "last updated" date on the page.

content.content:
# Text-to-Speech Leaderboard Summary

## Top Models & ELO Scores

1. **Fun-Realtime-TTS** - 1224
2. **Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS** - 1222
3. **Realtime TTS-2 (Research Preview)** - 1212
4. **Sonic 3.5** - 1203
5. **xAI Text to Speech** - 1200

## Models Mentioned from Your List

**Kokoro 82M v1.0**: ELO 1066; noted as "most affordable at $0.65 per 1M characters"

**StyleTTS 2**: Pricing of "$2.82 per 1M characters" mentioned as an affordable option

No mentions found for: XTTS, Piper, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Zonos

## Open-Source Leaders

The site identifies "Fish Audio S2 Pro" as "the highest-ranked open weights model" with ELO 1117, from 15 open-source options among 84 total models.

## Ranking Methodology

The leaderboard uses an "Elo rating system derived from user votes in blind comparisons in the Speech Arena." Users compare speech samples and vote on naturalness; "Higher Elo scores indicate a model produces speech preferred more often by listeners."

## Publication Date

No last-updated date visible in provided content.

content.text: Let me get more detail on the open-weights models and their relative rankings to extract more precise claims.

input.prompt: Focus on open-weights / open-source models. List every open-weights model shown with its exact ELO score. In particular report the exact ELO for: Fish Audio S2 Pro, Kokoro 82M v1.0, StyleTTS 2, and any others marked as open weights. State the ELO gap between the top open-weights model and Kokoro. Also quote exactly how the page describes the Speech Arena voting methodology and the total number of models and open-weights models evaluated.

content.content:
# Open-Weights Text-to-Speech Models Analysis

## Models with ELO Scores

Based on the content provided, here are the open-weights models explicitly listed with their exact ELO ratings:

1. **Fish Audio S2 Pro** - 1117
2. **Step Audio EditX (Mar 2026)** - 1115
3. **Voxtral TTS** - 1081
4. **Kokoro 82M v1.0** - 1066
5. **Magpie-Multilingual 357M (Feb 2026)** - 1065

StyleTTS 2 is mentioned as affordable at $2.82 per 1M characters but no ELO score is provided in this content.

## ELO Gap

The performance gap between the top open-weights model (Fish Audio S2 Pro at 1117) and Kokoro 82M v1.0 (1066) is **51 points**.

## Total Model Count

The page states there are **15 open weights models out of 84 total**.

## Voting Methodology

The page describes the approach as follows: *"Models are ranked using an Elo rating system derived from user votes in blind comparisons in the Speech Arena. Users listen to pairs of speech samples generated from the same text and choose which sounds more natural."*

content.text: I have sufficient detail to extract claims. The page is the Artificial Analysis Speech Arena leaderboard, a recognized independent benchmark using crowdsourced blind A/B preference voting (ELO).

claims.claim: On the Artificial Analysis Speech Arena leaderboard (independent crowdsourced blind A/B preference ELO), Kokoro 82M v1.0 scores 1066 ELO, ranking it below several open-weights TTS models including Fish Audio S2 Pro (1117), Step Audio EditX (1115), and Voxtral TTS (1081).
claims.quote: Fish Audio S2 Pro - 1117 ... Step Audio EditX (Mar 2026) - 1115 ... Voxtral TTS - 1081 ... Kokoro 82M v1.0 - 1066

claims.claim: The highest-ranked open-weights TTS model on the leaderboard is Fish Audio S2 Pro at 1117 ELO, giving it a 51-point ELO advantage over the Kokoro-82M baseline, indicating it is preferred more often by listeners in blind naturalness comparisons.
claims.quote: The performance gap between the top open-weights model (Fish Audio S2 Pro at 1117) and Kokoro 82M v1.0 (1066) is 51 points.

claims.claim: The leaderboard ranking reflects human-judged naturalness via blind pairwise listening tests rather than vendor-reported MOS, making it an independent evaluation: users compare same-text speech samples and vote on which sounds more natural, aggregated into an ELO rating.
claims.quote: Models are ranked using an Elo rating system derived from user votes in blind comparisons in the Speech Arena. Users listen to pairs of speech samples generated from the same text and choose which sounds more natural.

claims.claim: The overall leaderboard is dominated by proprietary/hosted models (e.g. Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS at 1222, Sonic 3.5 at 1203, xAI Text to Speech at 1200), all scoring well above any open-weights model, so the best naturalness is not currently available in a local/open model.
claims.quote: Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS - 1222 ... Sonic 3.5 - 1203 ... xAI Text to Speech - 1200

claims.claim: Open-weights options are a minority of evaluated systems (15 open-weights models out of 84 total), and Kokoro is positioned as the most affordable model on the board at $0.65 per 1M characters.
claims.quote: 15 open weights models out of 84 total ... Kokoro 82M v1.0: ELO 1066; noted as 'most affordable at $0.65 per 1M characters'

content.text:
I have completed the source extraction. The page at https://artificialanalysis.ai/text-to-speech/leaderboard/selected-voice is the Artificial Analysis Speech Arena TTS leaderboard.

Summary of findings:

Source quality: secondary (an independent third-party benchmark aggregator; the ELO scores are derived from crowdsourced human blind A/B preference votes, not vendor self-reports, but it is an aggregator rather than a primary peer-reviewed study).

Key takeaways relevant to the research question:
- Kokoro 82M v1.0 = 1066 ELO, beaten by several open-weights models on perceived naturalness.
- Top open-weights model is Fish Audio S2 Pro at 1117 ELO (+51 over Kokoro). Other open-weights models above Kokoro: Step Audio EditX (1115, Mar 2026), Voxtral TTS (1081). Magpie-Multilingual 357M (1065) sits roughly even with Kokoro.
- The board is dominated by proprietary/hosted models (Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS 1222, Sonic 3.5 1203, xAI TTS 1200), all well above any open model.
- 15 open-weights models out of 84 total; Kokoro flagged as cheapest at $0.65/1M chars.
- Methodology = blind pairwise "which sounds more natural" voting aggregated into ELO (independent human evaluation, not MOS).

Caveats for the parent agent: StyleTTS2 is referenced only by price, not by ELO. Several candidate models in the research scope (XTTS, Piper, F5-TTS, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Zonos) were not found on this page. No explicit "last updated" date was visible, though model entries dated Feb/Mar 2026 imply currency as of early-mid 2026. The page provides naturalness rankings only — it does not address word/phoneme timing, alignment output, high-speed intelligibility, or Apple Silicon local-inference feasibility.
